summary: This lesson covers the irregular preterite of 'dar' (to give), the phonetic rule that changes 'le lo' to 'se lo' when two l-words appear together as object pronouns, and how to use 'a + pronoun' for clarification/emphasis when sentences with 'se' are ambiguous.

teachings.summary: Dar is an -ar verb but takes -er/-ir endings in the preterite. It is the only verb that switches groups this way in the preterite.
teachings.rule: Dar in the preterite: di, dio, dimos. Note: 'dio' has no written accent (unlike regular -er/-ir preterite forms like comió).

teachings.conceptName: Se lo phonetic change (le lo -> se lo)
teachings.summary: When two object pronouns beginning with 'l' would appear together (le lo, les lo, le los, les los, le la, etc.), the first one changes to 'se'. This is purely a phonetic change -- Spanish does not like the sound of two l-words together.
teachings.rule: Any combination of le/les + lo/la/los/las becomes se + lo/la/los/las. This 'se' has no meaning change -- it is just a phonetic substitution.
teachings.notes: This 'se' is totally different from the reflexive 'se' (himself/herself). It is just a phonetic issue.

teachings.summary: When sentences with 'se lo' are ambiguous (could mean to him/her/them/you formal), you can clarify by adding 'a él', 'a ella', 'a ellos', 'a ellas', 'a usted', 'a ustedes' at the end. The structure is a repetition -- you keep the 'se' and add the clarification as an afterthought.
teachings.rule: With 'a' for clarification, always repeat the pronoun (le di algo a él -- keep the 'le' and add 'a él'). With other prepositions like 'para', do NOT repeat (lo compré para ti -- no 'me' needed).

examples.spanish: Él se lo envió a ella.

examples.english: He sent it to you guys.

examples.spanish: No sé si se lo envié.
examples.english: I don't know if I sent it to him.

teachings.notes: The 'a' used for clarification can represent other prepositions besides 'to' -- e.g., 'me lo compra a mí' could mean 'from me' or 'for me'. Because of this special flexible use, we always repeat with 'a'.
